The first part is to show that the support of a continuous distribution function F is a perfect set, which means it's closed and has no isolated points. Also, this support should coincide with the set of growth points G_F of F. The second part is to provide an example of a discrete distribution function F whose support is the entire real line R.

Starting with the first part. Let me recall some definitions. A distribution function F on R is a function that is non-decreasing, right-continuous, and satisfies lim_{x→-∞} F(x) = 0 and lim_{x→+∞} F(x) = 1. The support of F, supp F, is the smallest closed set G such that the measure μ corresponding to F has μ(R \ G) = 0. So, the support is the complement of the union of all open sets with measure zero. 

A perfect set is a closed set with no isolated points. So, I need to show that supp F is closed (which it is, since it's defined as the smallest closed set) and that it has no isolated points. Also, need to show that this support coincides with the set of growth points G_F.

In other words, for every ε > 0, there exists some y in (x - ε, x + ε) such that F(y) ≠ F(x). So, growth points are points where the distribution function actually increases, not just stays flat.
